dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit [UNIQUE_IDENTIFIER], priority 3 for a diabetic problem at a nursing facility in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Daniel, an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought EMS to your care today?
The patient has a diabetic issue. His blood sugar is high, and he's been acting strangely for about an hour.
Can you point to the patient or tell me his name?
Does he open his eyes when you speak to him?
Yes, but he's not making much sense-mostly just nodding or staying silent.
Does he follow commands at all, such as a simple nod or shake of the head?
Yes, he can do that, but his responses are slow and inconsistent.
His airway is patent with no visible obstruction or abnormal sounds.
Breathing is normal, spontaneous, and there is no obvious distress.
His skin is pink, warm, and dry with normal turgor.
What did the staff say about the patient's pupils and head condition?
Pupils were equal, round, 3 mm. No signs of trauma or injury. HEENT appeared normal.
He was found to have acute altered mental status. His GCS was 13.
No tracheal deviation or jugular vein distention observed.
His pulse is 106. Let me hook up the ECG now.
He's breathing at 18 per minute, shallow but regular.
Blood pressure is currently 112 over 78.
Oxygen is at 98%, which is good for now.
When did the strange behavior begin? Have you noticed any pattern or trigger?

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: About an hour before we called 911. He was given some insulin at 4:30, and he only had minor changes after that.
Has there been an increase in urination recently? Any possible signs of infection?
Yes, he's been acting more polyuric, and the staff suspects a possible UTI.
Could you share his chronic conditions or long-term health history?
Type one diabetes, dementia, anxiety, hyperlipidemia, and a history of manic episodes.
What medications does he regularly take?
Medications include Bisacodyl, fluoxetine, metformin, Levemir, and olanzapine, among others.
On a scale from 1 to 10, is this a painful issue for the patient?
No pain reported-just confusion, and he's not walking as usual.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; EMT
dialogue.utterance: Any signs of drainage, obstruction, or blockages in the nose, ear, or throat?

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Bystander
dialogue.utterance: No, no drainage or blockage reported by staff.
Chest is symmetric, atraumatic, and without any signs of trauma or bruising.
Breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally with no wheezes or crackles.
Any pelvic pain or swelling we should take note of?
No, he was examined and showed no abnormalities there.
He has a rash on the right forearm; otherwise skin looks normal.
Skin is pale in some patches but overall pink and dry.
The EKG is reading sinus rhythm-no other irregularities noted.
We'll attempt to start an IV in the left forearm. Let me try inserting it now.
First IV attempt in the left arm was unsuccessful.
Trying the right arm next. Second IV attempt also failed.
Based on vitals and altered mental status, we're following protocol 3-7 for diabetic hyperglycemia.
Reassessing vitals now. Pulse is 99, BP is 108 over 92, and SpO2 is 96%.
Does the nursing staff have a preferred destination for transport?
[COMPANY_NAME].
We'll transfer him from his wheelchair to the stretcher using a two-person carry.
Stretcher is secured and ready for transport.
Unit [UNIQUE_IDENTIFIER] is en route to [COMPANY_NAME].
Vital signs monitored continuously with no incidents during the move.
We'll move him from the stretcher to the hospital bed using a sheet pull upon arrival.
Patient exhibits altered mental status, with an initial BGL of 369 and read as 0 in reassessment.
Two IV attempts in left forearm and right arm were both unsuccessful.
All events occurred without incident, and transport followed nursing staff's instructions.
Transport based on field impression of a possible hyperglycemic crisis.
Protocol MED-3-7 for diabetic hyperglycemia was followed as per standing orders.
No signs of breathing issues or airway obstructions were present upon assessment.
Airway was patent, skin was pink, warm, and dry with normal turgor.
Mental status showed confusion; responded to simple commands using head movements.
His verbal and motor responses were consistent with GCS 13.
No signs of hypotension or hypertension-BP remained stable during transport.
Patient is on Levemir, metformin, olanzapine, and other medications for chronic conditions.
No drug allergies noted, so treatments like Levemir were appropriate.
No visible irregularities in his abdomen or pelvis.
No complications were observed in head movements or responses during care.
On Meds: bisacodyl, fluoxetine, olanzapine, metformin, and Levemir.
Blood glucose was 369 at 18:58 and read as 0 at 19:17.
The patient was alert enough to respond using basic yes/no commands.
HEENT was normal-no signs of trauma or injury detected.
EKG showed sinus rhythm with no changes between readings.
Patient had no signs of pain at any point.
He has a history of type one diabetes, dementia, anxiety, and hyperlipidemia.
Vital signs were consistent at both initial and reassessment-no worsening signs.
No abnormal skin color, respiratory, or circulatory signs.
No signs of hallucinations or visual disturbances.
No motor movements were observed-only head nods and silence.
No swelling or bruising was noted on the body.
All steps taken were in line with the medical record and EPCR documentation.
No incidents or patient issues during the entire transfer.
Documentation is complete and on file.
Patient now under care of hospital staff in ED Room 6.
No allergic reactions or adverse events reported during treatment.
All actions were part of standard medical procedures documented in EPCR.
Report fully delivered to hospital staff with all care details and protocol followed.
EMS care was completed with the hospital assuming responsibility.
Patient safely transported with full care transition and no delays.
No additional care or EMS action is required at this time.
All documentation and procedures were successfully transferred.
No outstanding concerns from nursing or EMS teams.
All baseline vitals were consistent and no adverse events were logged.
Unit [UNIQUE_IDENTIFIER] is available for the next call.
Any additional units awaiting status or feedback?
There are no other calls logged for this unit.
